question: Where can I get help or ask questions?
answer:
The best place to get help, ask questions, and connect with the AI Horde community is our Discord server. It's the most active community hub where you can:

  • Ask questions and get quick responses from experienced users and developers
  • Report issues or bugs you encounter
  • Share your creations and get feedback
  • Learn tips and tricks from other community members
  • Stay updated with the latest news and announcements
  • Participate in bounties and community events

You can also browse the available frontends and tools, read our documentation, or open issues on our GitHub repositories.

question: How does this all work?
answer:
The AI Horde is a free community service that connects people who need AI-generated content with volunteers who share their computer power. Think of it like Folding@home or SETI@home, but for generative AI.

When you submit a request (for example, "a painting of a sunset over mountains"), the system finds available volunteer computers (workers) that can process your job. Your request is queued based on your kudos balance—users with more kudos get faster service, but anyone can use it for free.

Workers continuously poll the horde for new jobs, generate the results, and return them to you. You can check active workers and their status on the active workers page, and explore available models on the models page.

question: Is this some sort of blockchain or cryptocurrency project?
answer:
No! The AI Horde is a completely centralized service that does not involve any blockchain technology or cryptocurrency. We explicitly reject the use of blockchain technologies and have no plans to integrate with them in any way.

Kudos, the priority system used in the horde, are a centralized point system that has no inherent monetary value and cannot be traded for currency. They are designed to encourage positive community behavior and resource sharing, not to function as a cryptocurrency.

question: Is this a doomed AI startup that will eventually run out of funding and shut down?
answer:
No! The AI Horde is a community-driven project run by a non-profit organization. We are registered in Luxembourg as a non-profit (Haidra, A.S.B.L.) dedicated to the advancement and accessibility of AI technologies. The project is designed to be sustainable and resilient. It is funded through a combination of donations, volunteer contributions, and support from organizations like NLnet / NGI0 Core (Next Generation Internet).

Since the horde relies on volunteer workers who contribute their computing resources for free, it has very low operating costs compared to traditional AI services. Even if funding were to decrease, the horde could continue to operate as long as there are volunteers willing to run workers.

Our commitment to open-source principles and community involvement also means that even if the original maintainers were to step back, the project could be continued by others in the community. We have a strong foundation and a passionate user base that supports the longevity of the AI Horde.

We do, however, have operating costs for infrastructure, and we rely on donations to cover these costs. If you find the AI Horde valuable, please consider supporting us through donations or by running a worker to contribute to the community!

question: Can I request or add a feature to the Horde?
answer: Yes! The AI Horde is a community project, and we welcome contributions. You can open an issue or a pull request to any of our repositories, or join us in our Discord server to discuss your ideas.

question: Where can I find more detailed technical information about the Horde?
answer:
We have a collection of markdown documents that provide additional context and information about the AI Horde, its architecture, and how it works.

Check out our GitHub organization for the source code behind the major components of the Horde. You can also explore the API documentation for detailed endpoint information.

question: What are Kudos?
answer:
Kudos are the priority system that determines your position in the queue when you make a request. The more kudos you have, the faster your requests will be processed. They are the fundamental unit of exchange of resources on the AI Horde.

When a worker completes a job, a value is assigned based on the job's difficulty, and the worker earns that many kudos. Workers also receive a nominal amount of kudos every 10 minutes just for being online and available, ensuring a steady pool of workers even when there aren't tasks to process.

Kudos never expire and can be freely exchanged between users, but cannot be sold for money—this is strictly against our Terms of Service. Anonymous users start with 0 kudos, while registered users have at least 25, giving them higher priority in the queue. If two users have the same kudos and submit a job at the same time, both jobs will return in about the same amount of time. Requests from users with no kudos are fulfilled on a first-come, first-serve basis.

Unspent kudos are effectively a donation to the community—workers who choose not to spend their kudos are donating their resources to the project, ensuring that users without kudos can still benefit from the platform.

Check the kudos leaderboard to see our top contributors, or read the full kudos documentation and this devlog about the kudos economy for more details.

question: How do I earn Kudos?
answer:
The best way to earn kudos is to run a worker! You will earn kudos for each request you fulfill based on its difficulty and complexity.

Additionally, workers earn uptime kudos every 10 minutes for staying online and available, even when not actively processing jobs. Workers that serve multiple models earn increased uptime kudos.

If you don't have the hardware to run a worker, see the "I don't have a powerful GPU" section for alternative ways to earn kudos.

question: How is image Kudos consumption calculated?
answer:
The kudos cost reflects the amount of processing required to generate the image.

Since each payload on the horde can have many variables affecting its speed, we have trained a neural network that takes a request payload as input and calculates the required kudos based on how much faster or slower it would generate compared to a baseline. The baseline is 10 kudos for a 50-step 512x512 image. If a payload is expected to take twice as long, it will cost 20 kudos.

The AI Horde API provides a dry_run payload key. When set to true, it returns the kudos cost for an image without actually generating it.

Additionally, there is a "horde tax" representing infrastructure costs for each request. These kudos are not received by the worker but are permanently burned:

  • 1 kudos tax per request (applied even if the request is cancelled, faulted, or aborted)
  • 1 kudos tax per job in a request
  • +20% kudos burn for requesting only fast workers
  • +10% kudos burn for requesting a worker blacklist

question: I don't have a powerful GPU. How can I earn Kudos?
answer:
Kudos are used to encourage positive behavior in the community. There are ways to earn kudos besides generating images for others (although that's the best way):

  • If you have at least 2GB VRAM, you can run an alchemist worker, which performs image interrogation and post-processing tasks.
  • Rate images. Most clients allow you to rate images while waiting, which earns kudos per rating. You can also rate images you generated for a kudos refund! Artbot has an easy rating page.
  • Fulfill a bounty from our Discord bounties forum.
  • Subscribe to the Patreon supporting AI Horde development (or use other donation methods).
  • Share cool art in our Discord.
  • Politely ask others to transfer kudos to you in our Discord server. People often give kudos to new users and those who are helpful or funny.

question: Can I sell my Kudos?
answer:
No! Kudos have no inherent monetary value, and we do not allow anyone to exchange kudos for money. This is a core principle of the AI Horde designed to prevent commodification and ensure equitable access to resources. Keeping kudos non-commercial prevents external financial pressures from distorting the system.

Attempting to bypass this rule is an existential threat to the AI Horde. If you exchange money for kudos and we discover it, we may zero out your account and the account of the person you bought from!

However, kudos can be freely exchanged between users for any reason other than for currency. For example, users may gift kudos to each other as a gesture of gratitude or to support new members of the community. You can transfer kudos here.

We do offer kudos as a thank-you if you donate; however, the kudos you receive are offset by kudos earned by workers run by the core developers. See our Terms of Service for the full policy.

question: Is Kudos a cryptocurrency?
answer: No! Kudos are completely centralized and do not involve any blockchain technology. The AI Horde is explicitly opposed to blockchain technologies and will never integrate with them. Likewise, there is no way to convert Kudos to anything other than favors that benefit the improvement of the AI Horde.

question: Can you explain how a typical new user interacts with Kudos?
answer:
Let's walk through a typical new user's journey:

Starting out as an anonymous user

Alex discovers AI Horde and generates a few images without registering. As an anonymous user with 0 kudos, Alex's requests are placed at the back of the queue and fulfilled on a first-come, first-serve basis alongside other zero-kudos users. A 512×512 image at 50 steps (the baseline, costing about 10 kudos) takes a few minutes depending on how busy the horde is. During peak times, requests may even time out and need to be resubmitted.

Registering an account

Alex registers and immediately is treated as having 25 kudos. This doesn't sound like much, but it matters: your starting place in the queue is determined by total kudos balance, so Alex now jumps ahead of all anonymous users and unregistered traffic. The same 512×512 image now arrives noticeably faster.

Deciding to run a worker

Alex has an RTX 3070 that sits idle most evenings and decides to set up a worker. Here's what happens:

  • The worker connects to the horde via the bridge software and starts polling for jobs.
  • Alex loads two popular Stable Diffusion models. Serving multiple models means the worker qualifies for higher uptime kudos every 10 minutes, even when no jobs are queued.
  • Each completed image job earns kudos proportional to its difficulty. That same baseline 512×512 / 50-step image earns the worker roughly 10 kudos. A more demanding 1024×1024 image at 80 steps would earn significantly more.
  • Over the course of an evening (say 4 hours), the worker might complete dozens of jobs and accumulate thousands of kudos (or even tens of thousands with high-end hardware), plus uptime kudos for every 10-minute interval it was online.

Spending kudos

The next day, Alex wants to generate a batch of 20 high-resolution images using a large model like SDXL. Each image might cost 25–40 kudos depending on resolution and steps. With thousands of kudos saved up, Alex's requests sit near the top of the queue and come back quickly. Importantly, each of those 20 images can be processed by a different worker in parallel, so the whole batch finishes much faster than generating them one-by-one locally.

The horde tax

Alex notices the kudos balance drops by a bit more than the raw image cost. That's because each request includes a small horde tax that covers infrastructure costs: 1 kudos per request, 1 kudos per job within a request, and additional surcharges if Alex requests only fast workers (+20%) or uses a worker blacklist (+10%). These taxed kudos are permanently burned and not paid to any worker.

Key things new users often miss

  • Your balance is never deducted below the minimum of 25. Even after heavy use, you can always submit requests—you'll just wait longer in the queue because your priority drops.
  • Kudos never expire. If Alex stops using the horde for months, the balance is still there.
  • You don't need a powerful GPU. Even a card with 2 GB VRAM can run an alchemist worker (image post-processing), and rating images earns kudos with no GPU at all.
  • Your own worker serves you first. While Alex's worker is running, any of Alex's own requests are automatically prioritized on that worker, regardless of kudos.
  • Unspent kudos help everyone. If Alex accumulates 10,000 kudos and never spends them, that's effectively a donation—it means Alex processed 10,000 kudos worth of work for others without taking anything back, keeping the system healthy for users who have no kudos.

question: Can you explain how a service operator or developer would interact with Kudos?
answer:
Let's walk through a typical developer's journey building a service on top of the AI Horde:

Discovering the API

Sam is a developer who wants to add AI image generation to a web app. Instead of provisioning expensive GPU servers, Sam discovers the AI Horde's free REST API. Sam registers an account and receives an API key. With the default 25 kudos, Sam can immediately start making API calls and prototyping.

Prototyping with API calls

Sam integrates the /v2/generate/async endpoint into the app. Each image request costs kudos based on its complexity—the baseline is 10 kudos for a 512×512, 50-step image. Sam uses the dry_run payload key to estimate costs before committing, which is useful for showing users an expected wait time or cost preview without actually queuing a job.

Proxying user requests

As more users try Sam's app, the typical pattern is to have the backend act as a proxy: users interact with Sam's website, and the backend forwards their requests to the AI Horde API using Sam's own API key—users never see or need a horde account. To properly attribute usage, Sam includes the proxied_account field on /v2/generate/async requests, which tags each job with the originating user. For this to work, Sam requests the service account flag on the account by asking in the Discord server. This way, all requests benefit from Sam's kudos balance and queue priority, while the horde can still track per-user activity for abuse prevention.

Running workers to sustain the service

To build up kudos, Sam sets up two workers on cloud GPU instances during off-peak hours when rates are cheap. The workers earn kudos for every job they complete—just like any other worker on the horde. Over a weekend, the workers accumulate hundreds of thousands of kudos, giving Sam's app a large balance and high queue priority for all its users.

The give-back expectation for paid services

Sam considers offering a premium tier with faster generation. The AI Horde allows this, and the community expectation is: give back to the horde at least as much as you take out. This isn't a binding contractual obligation—it's good manners, and frankly it's just how the system naturally works. The AI Horde is built on goodwill and altruism, and the kudos system is self-limiting: if Sam's service consumes more kudos than it contributes, the account's balance drops, queue priority falls, and users lose access to certain generation parameters restricted when you have a zero-kudos accounts. In practice, running workers to replenish kudos is simply what it takes to keep the service running well. See the detailed policy on paid integrations.

Monitoring and managing costs

Sam uses the API's status endpoints to monitor worker performance, check the app's kudos balance, and track consumption. The horde tax still applies: 1 kudos per request, 1 kudos per job, plus surcharges for fast-worker-only (+20%) or blacklist (+10%) requests. Sam factors these into the app's cost estimates using dry_run.

Key things developers often miss

  • Proxy with a service account for multi-user apps. Have your backend proxy requests using your API key with the proxied_account field. Request the service account flag in the Discord so per-user attribution works correctly.
  • Workers you run serve your own requests first. If Sam's workers are online, all requests from Sam's app are automatically prioritized on those workers—a significant advantage for response times.
  • Concurrency is your superpower. Unlike a single local GPU, the horde can process many requests in parallel across different workers. A batch of 50 images can return much faster than generating them sequentially.
  • No vendor lock-in. The API is open, the code is FOSS, and you can even run a private horde instance if your needs outgrow the public one.
  • Kudos never expire. If Sam pauses the service for months, the accumulated kudos balance remains intact.

question: What is a worker?
answer:
A worker is a computer, usually with a mid-range or higher GPU, that has installed specific software to generate images via Stable Diffusion or text via Large Language Models locally.

It connects to the horde through what we call the "bridge"—a software component that handles communication between the worker and the AI Horde API. The worker constantly polls the horde for new jobs and receives kudos in return for completing them.

There are three main types of workers:

  • Dreamers: Image generation workers using Stable Diffusion, SDXL, Flux, and more
  • Scribes: Text generation workers using Large Language Models
  • Alchemists: Image processing workers for tasks like upscaling, face-fixing, and image interrogation

Visit our becoming a worker page to learn how to set up your own worker.

question: What do workers gain from participating?
answer:
Generating images for others 24/7 does consume electricity, which we acknowledge.

People contribute to the horde for many reasons:

  • Converting GPU downtime to value: If your GPU isn't in constant use, you can "bank" kudos during idle periods. If your worker is configured with popular models, it will receive a continuous stream of work. You can then use those kudos later when you need faster or higher-end services.
  • Converting lower-end GPUs to higher-end results: Lower-end GPUs are still valuable to many users, but may only run smaller models. Accumulate kudos with a modest GPU, then use them to request generations from more powerful hardware capable of running larger models.
  • Improved concurrency: Each request is processed by a separate worker. If you need more results per second than your GPU provides, build up kudos and make multiple requests simultaneously—for example, splitting a batch of 50 images across many workers to dramatically reduce total generation time.
  • Building applications: Developers can power their own Stable Diffusion or KoboldAI-based tools using the horde infrastructure.
  • Productive heating: Yes, some people really do use it to warm their rooms!
  • Altruism: Most kudos earned by workers go unspent. Unspent kudos are effectively a donation to the community, ensuring users without kudos can still benefit from the platform. Workers may also have ideological reasons such as supporting open-source or promoting decentralized AI.

Whether you're a content creator, developer, or researcher, earned kudos let you access a wide range of models and resources you might not have locally. Read more about our motivations for running this service, or see the full kudos documentation for details.

question: Can workers see my prompts or generations?
answer:
Technically, yes. While the worker software and bridge code are not designed to allow this, the software runs on someone else's computer and is open source. Anyone with the know-how can modify their code to view all prompts passing through or save the results they generate.

However, workers do not have any identifying information about individual requestors—they cannot see your user ID, username, or IP address. The system is designed to protect user privacy while allowing workers to process jobs.

As a best practice, always request generations as if you were posting in a public forum, like using a Discord bot. While the horde provides reasonable privacy protections, treating requests as potentially public is a good security habit.

question: Can I turn off my worker whenever I want?
answer:
Yes! Workers are not required to stay online at all times. We only ask that you put your worker into maintenance mode before shutting it down to avoid interrupting any jobs it may be processing.

When in maintenance mode, your worker finishes its current jobs but doesn't accept new ones. You can manage your workers from your profile page.

question: Do workers support multiple models?
answer:
Yes! Workers can be configured to serve multiple models simultaneously. When you make a request, you can specify which model you want to use, or leave the model unspecified to receive the first available model from any worker that picks up your job.

Workers that serve multiple models earn increased uptime kudos (awarded every 10 minutes), reflecting their higher value to the community. Check the models page to see which models are currently available.

question: Can I prioritize myself and my friends on my own worker?
answer: Yes! By default, your own requests are always served first by your worker, regardless of kudos. You can also specify certain usernames to be prioritized in the same way.

question: Why is it called "AI Horde"?
answer: This project began as a way to consolidate resources for the KoboldAI Client. We needed a name, so I chose something thematic for "Kobolds": "A Horde of Kobolds." When image generation was added later, we kept the "AI Horde" name.

question: Can you explain the terminology used in the AI Horde?
answer: We have a comprehensive glossary that explains and cross-references terms commonly used when discussing the AI Horde, including concepts like workers, dreamers, scribes, alchemists, kudos, and more.

question: Does the horde spy on my prompts and generations?
answer:
No, the AI Horde itself does not store such details for surveillance or data collection purposes. Prompts and generations are only stored in memory temporarily during processing and are deleted shortly after the generation is delivered or canceled.

The system retains requests only long enough to facilitate job processing and result retrieval. Once you've retrieved your results or the request expires, the data is permanently removed from our systems. Read our privacy policy for more details.

question: Why should I use the Horde instead of my local PC?
answer:
The AI Horde democratizes access to generative AI technologies. Not everyone has a powerful GPU in their PC, and the horde allows anyone to use fast Stable Diffusion and Large Language Models, regardless of their hardware or financial means.

Additional benefits include:

  • Accessibility: No expensive graphics cards required—use it even on your phone through browser-based frontends.
  • Easy Setup: No complex Python dependencies or error-prone local installations to manage.
  • Bandwidth Savings: No need to download large models (which can be gigabytes in size).
  • Developer-Friendly: If you're building a service on image or text generation, you can use the horde infrastructure without the complexity and costs of setting up your own servers.

Read more about our motivations and commitment to accessibility.

question: Why should I use the Horde instead of a service like Stability.ai?
answer:
Because the AI Horde is completely free! You will never need to pay to use the horde. If demand is high, your delivery speed may be slower, but that's also true for other services like Midjourney.

Second, the horde gives you all the benefits of a local installation—such as prompt freedom and model choice—while still providing a convenient browser interface and flexibility.

Finally, unlike many commercial services, the horde provides a fully-fledged REST API you can use to integrate your applications without worrying about ongoing costs or usage limits.

question: Why should I use the Horde instead of a free service?
answer:
Because when the service is free, you're the product!

Other services running on centralized servers have costs—someone has to pay for electricity and infrastructure. The AI Horde is transparent about how these costs are crowdsourced through volunteer workers, and there is no need for us to change our model in the future.

Other "free" services are often vague about how they use your data or explicitly state that your data is the product. Such services may eventually monetize through advertising, data brokering, or subscription paywalls. The AI Horde operates on a not-for-profit basis and is committed to remaining free and open-source.

Additionally, many of these services do not provide free REST APIs. If you need to integrate with them, you must use a browser interface designed to serve ads or collect data.

question: Can I run my own private horde?
answer: Of course! This software is FOSS (Free and Open Source), and you are welcome to use, modify, and share it, as long as you respect the AGPL3 license.

question: Can I build paid service integrations into the AI Horde?
answer:
Yes, with important restrictions. Due to the voluntary nature of the AI Horde, you must give back to the AI Horde at least as much as you take out to make a profit. This ensures that paid services don't extract value from volunteer contributions without reciprocating.

In practice, this means if you're building a paid service on top of the horde, you should run workers that contribute at least as many kudos as your paid users consume. This maintains the balance and fairness of the system.

Please see the detailed explanation in this devlog for specific guidelines and examples.

question: Can I request NSFW images or text?
answer: Yes, but you may have a smaller pool of workers to fulfill your request, which can result in longer generation times. Check the active workers page to see how many workers are currently serving NSFW requests.

question: Do you censor generations?
answer:
Horde-wide, we censor only one type of generation: CSAM (Child Sexual Abuse Material) images. We use multiple mechanisms to prevent this: a regex replacement filter during the initial API request and an anti-CSAM AI running on each worker. See the detailed answers below for more information.

Other than CSAM prevention, the horde does not censor text generations or images. We are committed to providing prompt freedom and open access to generative AI technologies.

However, individual workers may have their own censorship guidelines based on their operator's preferences or local laws, and each requestor can voluntarily opt in to accidental NSFW filtering.

question: Why are some of my images just black with white text?
answer:
Those generations have been NSFW-censored by the worker that generated them.

If you specified your request as SFW (Safe For Work), individual SFW workers who fulfill it may have an NSFW censorship model active, which will return this black warning image if it detects potentially explicit content. To avoid such images, enable NSFW in your request settings or ensure your prompt is clearly SFW.

If the image is censored due to the anti-CSAM filter, this cannot be disabled as it is a mandatory safety measure across all workers.

question: Why are some of my images still censored even though I'm requesting NSFW?
answer:
Each worker can optionally define a censor list. If any word from that list is found in your prompt, the worker will automatically apply post-processing using an NSFW censorship model.

These censor lists typically contain terms that should never be combined with NSFW content or could cause legal issues for the worker operator based on their local laws.

If your images are censored, you can try resubmitting the prompt to potentially get a different worker, or consider adjusting your prompt. If you believe a worker is using the censor list maliciously or improperly, please contact us with your prompt and the worker's name via our Discord server, and we'll investigate.

question: Can you explain how the Anti-CSAM regex filter works?
answer:
When an image request first enters the AI Horde, it passes through a private regex filter that looks for a combination of two contexts: "Underage" and "Lewd." For example, "child" is an underage context, and "without clothes" is a lewd context.

If neither or only one of these contexts is detected in a prompt, the prompt is allowed through. For example, "child in the playground" is fine, as is "without clothes in the bathroom."

If both contexts are present, the regex protection is triggered with two possible responses:

  • Replacement Mode: If replacement_filter is true in your API payload and the prompt is under 1000 characters, each triggering term is automatically replaced with an "adult" version (e.g., "school" becomes "university"). This aims to guide the inference toward safe content.
  • Block Mode: If replacement_filter is false or your prompt exceeds 1000 characters, the request is automatically blocked, and your IP receives a temporary timeout for a few minutes. This timeout increases with repeated offenses to prevent attempts to reverse-engineer the filter.

question: Can you explain how the Anti-CSAM AI works?
answer: We have written a detailed devlog about this.

question: Where can I read more about NSFW controls?
answer:
• The NSFW Question
  • Blacklists
